main_summary.summary: Ellie Dahmer, widow of Vernon Dahmer Sr., recounts her life in the Kelly Settlement of Forrest County, Mississippi, and her family's central role in voter-registration organizing in Hattiesburg. Trained at Alcorn and Tennessee A&I, she earned a bachelor's degree in home economics in 1947 and taught in Jasper, Jones, and Forrest Counties before being blacklisted following school consolidation at Earl Travillion. She describes meeting Vernon Dahmer, raising their children on the family farm and sawmill, and trying repeatedly to register to vote with Forrest County registrar Theron Lynd, who refused to issue cards to qualified Black applicants. Ellie was one of the 18 plaintiffs in U.S. v. Lynd, the Justice Department case argued by John Doar; her husband's voter-registration card arrived only in time to be buried with him after the KKK firebombing of their home on 10 January 1966. She hosted NAACP meetings in her living room, sheltered SNCC organizers Curtis Hayes (Curtis Muhammad) and Hollis Watkins from McComb, cooked for Freedom Summer 1964 volunteers, and later served three terms as a Forrest County election commissioner.
main_summary.historical_significance:
Ellie Dahmer is a first-person witness to the Forrest County voter-registration campaign at its center: she names Theron Lynd's literacy-test procedure, the affidavit notarization required of Black teachers who gave money to the NAACP, and the U.S. v. Lynd trial evidence that two Black women who appeared white were registered without testing, proving Lynd's disparate-treatment intent. She is also the sole surviving narrator of life inside the Dahmer household during the months leading to the January 1966 firebombing, including the family's practice of sleeping in shifts and her own work sewing blackout curtains.

The interview documents the systematic draft-targeting of NAACP-family sons (her testimony that Alvin was drafted on crutches), the role of the Kelly Settlement as a hosting site for SNCC organizers Curtis Hayes and Hollis Watkins from McComb, and the Freedom Summer 1964 watermelon-picnic incident in which a young SNCC tractor-driver inadvertently rode through the white side of Hattiesburg. Her later service as a Forrest County election commissioner gives the interview a post-Movement closing arc tied directly to the Voting Rights Act of 1965.

chapters.title: Alcorn to Tennessee A&I
chapters.summary: Ellie Dahmer describes her undergraduate years at Alcorn in the early 1940s and her transfer to Tennessee A&I to complete a bachelor's degree. She recounts the dean at Tennessee A&I requiring her to repeat Alcorn coursework, contrasting the resource gap between Black schools and the city schools and law schools that white students attended.

chapters.title: Teaching in Forrest County
chapters.summary: After graduating in 1947, Ellie taught home economics in Jasper County, Jones County, and then Forrest County, moving for better salary. She describes the disparity in school resources, the literacy test the white superintendent gave Black teaching applicants, and the consolidation that built Earl Travillion as the single Black school for Forrest County, after which she was named on a list of teachers not to rehire.

chapters.title: Meeting Vernon Dahmer
chapters.summary: Ellie recounts being recruited to teach in Kelly Settlement and meeting Vernon Dahmer there. She describes his Christian practice, his ownership of land in Kelly Settlement, the family farm and sawmill operations, the grocery store on the property, and his interest in making things better for other people.

chapters.title: Family Farm and Children
chapters.summary: Ellie describes the working farm operations: tractors replacing horses, six boys and one daughter Bettie helping in the fields, children learning to drive young. She names crops (cotton, corn, peanuts, sugar cane) and the grocery store, and notes she handled sales-tax paperwork and Social Security records for sawmill workers.

chapters.title: Trying to Register with Theron Lynd
chapters.summary: Ellie describes her early attempts to register to vote in Hattiesburg with registrar Theron Lynd, who handed her a card with part of the Constitution and rejected her interpretation regardless of accuracy. She explains the affidavit teachers were required to notarize disclosing organizations they donated to, with NAACP a forbidden recipient.

chapters.title: Voter Card Buried With Him
chapters.summary: Ellie states that Vernon Dahmer never had a chance to vote in his lifetime; his voter-registration card arrived from the Justice Department only in time to be buried with him. She describes her own later voting practice of going to the polling place except for one absentee vote at the courthouse after a hip replacement.

chapters.title: Three Terms as Election Commissioner
chapters.summary: Ellie describes running for and serving three terms as election commissioner for District 2 of Forrest County roughly ten to fifteen years after Vernon's death. She recounts gathering qualifying signatures with her daughter and others, working alongside two Black commissioners and one white colleague, and following the law to ensure no one was denied the vote.

chapters.title: NAACP Meetings in the Living Room
chapters.summary: Ellie describes NAACP meetings held in the Dahmer living room with a lookout posted by the car, the local chief of police arriving at the house on one occasion, and a small core of roughly ten neighbors willing to be known as NAACP members. She names Mrs. Beard as the lead worker with young people in the local NAACP branch, including in connection with the Ladner sisters (Dorie Ann and Joyce).

chapters.title: Living Under Threat
chapters.summary: Ellie describes the climate of fear preceding the firebombing: receiving threats, hives breaking out on her neck, sleeping in shifts with the children dressed for school, sewing blackout curtains so figures could not be seen through the window, and the family keeping the front porch light off. She notes the KKK posted signs on utility poles that Vernon would tear down, and that the sheriff was complicit or related to participants.

chapters.title: U.S. v. Lynd: The 18 Plaintiffs
chapters.summary: Ellie identifies herself as one of the 18 plaintiffs the Justice Department forced Theron Lynd to register without requiring them to refile. She recounts the trial evidence that two Black women in the Beard family who appeared white were allowed to register without being tested, proving Lynd's intent to discriminate. She names herself, her aunt, her mother-in-law, and others as the 18.
related_events: U.S. v. Lynd federal voting-rights case

chapters.title: John Doar and the Federal Court
chapters.summary: Ellie describes Justice Department attorney John Doar handling the case in a federal court hearing before a three-judge panel, distinguishing it from later proceedings before Judge W. Harold Cox. She recounts that a young Dahmer son was nearly jailed for taking a photograph during the proceedings and that Doar's strategy spared most plaintiffs from having to testify in person.

chapters.title: Hosting Curtis Hayes and Hollis Watkins
chapters.summary: Ellie recounts that Vernon came back from a Jackson NAACP meeting with SNCC organizers Curtis Hayes (Curtis Muhammad) and Hollis Watkins out of McComb. The two young men lived with the Dahmer family, worked alongside the children, and after their work canvassed the community for voter registration through SNCC and COFO.

chapters.title: Freedom Summer Picnic
chapters.summary: Ellie describes her work cooking for the Freedom Summer 1964 volunteers at a celebratory picnic on the Dahmer property: potato salad, fish, and watermelons retrieved from the field. She recounts the moment a young SNCC tractor-driver returned through the white community in Hattiesburg with the watermelon trailer behind him, an unintended danger.

chapters.title: Drafted Sons and the Voting Rights Act
chapters.summary: Ellie recounts that her older sons were systematically targeted for the draft as soon as they turned 18, including Alvin who was taken on crutches. Vernon Jr., Joe volunteered; Hal and Alvin were drafted. She closes with her reaction to the 1965 Voting Rights Act and the federal registrars working from a separate booth because Black registrants were not allowed inside the Forrest County courthouse.
